methodology_sections: 1.2 Trust Boundary Analysis - Internal/external/privileged boundaries, validation levels
2.2 State Transition Mapping - Critical state variables, invariants, conditional paths
3.1 Vulnerability Pattern Matching - SWC patterns, anti-patterns, missing safeguards

failure_modes: Unauthorized users can execute privileged operations
Theft of funds via malicious FeeSplitter or Curves contract
attack_vectors: Direct call to onlyOwner/onlyManager functions by any address due to broken modifiers
Direct call to setCurves in FeeSplitter to hijack fee distribution

failure_modes: Loss of user fees on balance change
Theft of fees via token transfer
Denial of Service via array bloat
Denial of Service via zero total supply
attack_vectors: Transferring tokens to a new address to claim unearned fees
Wrapping all tokens to ERC20 to halt trading
Repeatedly buying/selling to bloat userTokens array

state_transitions.from_state: User has ETH, Contract has Token Supply
state_transitions.to_state: User has Tokens, Contract has ETH, Fees Distributed
conditions: Sale is open or user is whitelisted
invariants: curvesTokenSupply equals sum of all curvesTokenBalance
ETH balance increases by price + totalFee minus distributed fees

state_transitions.to_state: User has ERC20 Tokens, Internal Tokens locked in Contract
conditions: User has sufficient internal token balance
invariants: ERC20 totalSupply equals internal tokens locked * 1 ether

vulnerability_patterns.evidence: Modifiers evaluate boolean expressions (msg.sender == owner;) without require() or revert(). setCurves lacks any access control modifier.
vulnerability_patterns.exploitation_method: An attacker can call any function protected by onlyOwner or onlyManager, or call setCurves to point to a malicious contract and drain funds.

vulnerability_patterns.evidence: The _transfer function does not update the userFeeOffset for the receiver. The receiver's offset remains 0.
vulnerability_patterns.exploitation_method: An attacker can buy tokens, transfer them to a new address, and immediately call claimFees from the new address. Since the offset is 0, the contract will calculate owed fees from the beginning of time, allowing the attacker to drain the FeeSplitter.

vulnerability_patterns.pattern: Code With No Effects / Logic Error
vulnerability_patterns.evidence: onBalanceChange overwrites data.userFeeOffset[account] with data.cumulativeFeePerToken without first calculating and storing the user's pending fees.
vulnerability_patterns.exploitation_method: Whenever a user buys or sells a token, onBalanceChange is called, and any unclaimed fees they had accumulated are permanently lost.

vulnerability_patterns.evidence: userTokens[account].push(token) is called on every buy/sell without checking if the token is already in the array.
vulnerability_patterns.exploitation_method: Repeatedly buying and selling a token will bloat the userTokens array, causing out-of-gas errors in getUserTokensAndClaimable and batchClaiming.

vulnerability_patterns.pattern: Denial of Service (Revert on Zero Supply)
vulnerability_patterns.evidence: addFees reverts if totalSupply_ == 0. totalSupply_ excludes tokens locked in the Curves contract (wrapped as ERC20).
vulnerability_patterns.exploitation_method: If all circulating tokens are wrapped into ERC20, addFees reverts, which causes _transferFees to revert, completely halting all buying and selling of the token.

vulnerability_patterns.evidence: During a sell, if referralDefined is false, the referralFee is subtracted from the sellValue but is never sent to the protocol or the user.
vulnerability_patterns.exploitation_method: Normal protocol usage will cause ETH to become permanently locked in the Curves contract.
